questions.stem: Which Snowflake architectural layer handles query compilation and optimization before sending work to the virtual warehouse?

options.text: Storage layer
Compute layer (virtual warehouse)
Cloud Services layer
Network ingress layer

questions.explanation: C is correct because the Cloud Services layer is responsible for query parsing, optimization, and compilation. It uses the metadata cache (containing table statistics and micro-partition metadata) to generate an efficient execution plan before dispatching work to the virtual warehouse. A is wrong because the storage layer persists micro-partitioned data in cloud object storage. B is wrong because the compute layer (virtual warehouse) executes the already-compiled query plan but does not compile it. D is wrong because 'network ingress layer' is not a Snowflake architectural component.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Cloud Services Layer, Query Optimization

questions.stem: Snowflake is available on which cloud platforms? (Choose the BEST answer)

options.text: AWS only
AWS and Microsoft Azure only
AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform
Any on-premises data center with supported hardware

questions.explanation: C is correct because Snowflake is available natively on all three major public cloud providers: AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform. Customers choose their cloud provider and region at account creation. A is wrong because Snowflake is not limited to AWS. B is wrong because GCP is also a supported platform. D is wrong because Snowflake is a fully managed cloud service and does not support on-premises deployment.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Cloud Provider Support

questions.stem: A Medium virtual warehouse (4 credits/hr) starts up, processes a single query, then is manually suspended exactly 90 seconds later. The warehouse had been fully suspended before this run. How many credits are consumed?

options.text: 4 credits (full hour billed)
0.1 credits (90 seconds at per-second rate)
0.067 credits (60-second minimum: 4 credits/hr × 1/60 hr)
0 credits because the query result cache handled the query

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake bills per second with a 60-second minimum. The warehouse ran for 90 seconds, which exceeds the 60-second minimum, so per-second billing applies: 4 credits/hr ÷ 3600 sec/hr × 90 sec = 0.1 credits. A is wrong because Snowflake does not bill for a full hour — per-second billing after a 60-second minimum is used. C is wrong because 60 seconds (the minimum) would apply only if the warehouse ran for less than 60 seconds; at 90 seconds, the actual runtime of 90 seconds is billed. D is wrong because the query result cache returns results without warehouse compute, but the warehouse itself was running for 90 seconds and those seconds are billed regardless of cache usage.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Virtual Warehouse Per-Second Billing, 60-Second Minimum

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes the Economy scaling policy for multi-cluster virtual warehouses?

options.text: Snowflake adds a new cluster as soon as any query is queued.
Snowflake adds a new cluster only when it estimates that the current cluster(s) will be busy for at least 6 minutes.
The Economy policy prevents Snowflake from ever removing clusters once they are added.
The Economy policy disables automatic scaling and requires manual cluster management.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Economy scaling policy is conservative — Snowflake waits until it predicts the queued load will keep an additional cluster busy for at least 6 minutes before provisioning it. This avoids spinning up clusters for brief spikes, trading some query latency for lower credit consumption. A is wrong because immediately provisioning on any queued query is the Standard (not Economy) scaling policy behavior. C is wrong because both policies remove clusters when demand drops. D is wrong because both Standard and Economy are automatic scaling policies.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Multi-Cluster Warehouse Economy Policy

questions.stem: A Snowflake table has 1 million rows. A developer runs `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM large_table`. Which cache is MOST LIKELY used to serve this query without involving the virtual warehouse?

options.text: Local disk cache
Query result cache
Metadata cache
External cache

questions.explanation: C is correct because `SELECT COUNT(*)` on a table is a metadata-only operation that Snowflake can satisfy from the Cloud Services layer's metadata cache, which stores row counts and other statistics about every table. No virtual warehouse is required. B is also a possibility if the exact query was run before within 24 hours, but the metadata cache specifically enables COUNT(*) to be answered without ever engaging the warehouse. A is wrong because the local disk cache is on the warehouse nodes and requires the warehouse to be active. D is wrong because 'external cache' is not a Snowflake concept.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Metadata Cache, Cloud Services Layer

questions.stem: A company on the Standard edition asks which features are NOT available to them. Which TWO features are exclusive to Enterprise edition and above? (Select TWO)

options.text: Time Travel retention period of up to 90 days
Multi-cluster warehouses
Data sharing with reader accounts
Fail-Safe data recovery

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. Time Travel retention of up to 90 days (Standard is capped at 1 day) and multi-cluster warehouses are both Enterprise edition features not available on Standard. C is wrong because data sharing and reader accounts are available on Standard edition — data sharing is a core Snowflake feature not gated to Enterprise. D is wrong because Fail-Safe (7-day recovery period) is available on all editions for standard tables.
questions.reference: Snowflake Editions — Standard vs Enterprise Feature Comparison

questions.stem: A Snowflake account is configured to use 'Standard' Time Travel (default 1-day retention). A table is dropped. How long does the administrator have to run UNDROP TABLE before the data is permanently unrecoverable by users?

options.text: 1 day (Time Travel expires, then Fail-Safe begins and is also user-accessible)
1 day (Time Travel only; after expiry, data moves to Fail-Safe which users cannot access)
8 days (1 day Time Travel + 7 day Fail-Safe, both accessible via UNDROP)
7 days (only Fail-Safe applies after a DROP TABLE)

questions.explanation: B is correct because UNDROP TABLE uses Time Travel data. Once the 1-day Time Travel retention expires, the user can no longer run UNDROP TABLE — the data enters Fail-Safe, which is managed only by Snowflake Support and is NOT accessible to users via SQL commands. So the effective window for user recovery is 1 day. A is wrong because Fail-Safe is NOT user-accessible. C is wrong because UNDROP TABLE cannot access Fail-Safe data. D is wrong because Fail-Safe begins after Time Travel expires, and neither period applies only to DROP TABLE specifically.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Protection — Time Travel vs Fail-Safe Accessibility

questions.stem: A Snowflake warehouse is set to AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 (seconds) and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. Multiple short queries arrive in rapid succession over a 2-hour period, with each burst lasting ~2 minutes followed by 5-minute idle gaps. What is the billing behavior?

options.text: The warehouse is billed continuously for 2 hours since it never fully suspends between bursts.
The warehouse suspends after each 60-second idle gap, and each resume incurs a new 60-second minimum charge.
The warehouse suspends after each 60-second idle period within the 5-minute gap, and each resume cycle incurs at least a 60-second minimum.
Credits are only billed for the 2-minute active bursts, and idle time is always free.

questions.explanation: C is correct. With AUTO_SUSPEND = 60, the warehouse suspends 60 seconds after the last query in a burst completes. With 5-minute idle gaps, the warehouse will suspend about 60 seconds into each idle period. When the next burst arrives, AUTO_RESUME wakes the warehouse, incurring at least a 60-second minimum charge for that activation cycle. Over 2 hours with multiple burst-idle cycles, this pattern means multiple 60-second minimums accumulate. A is wrong because the 5-minute idle gaps exceed the 60-second auto-suspend threshold, so the warehouse does suspend repeatedly. B describes the mechanism correctly but is slightly imprecise about when suspension occurs — it is 60 seconds after the last query, not after each query individually. D is wrong because idle time before auto-suspend (the 60-second window) IS billed.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Auto-Suspend Billing, 60-Second Minimum

questions.stem: A new custom role `REPORTING` is created and granted to a user. For the user to use this role to query a table in `mydb.analytics`, which privileges are required at a minimum?

options.text: USAGE on the database and schema only
SELECT on the table only
USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and SELECT on the table
OWNERSHIP on the database, schema, and table

questions.explanation: C is correct because in Snowflake's RBAC model, to query a table, a role needs USAGE on the database (to see the database), USAGE on the schema (to see the schema), and SELECT on the table. All three are required — missing any one of them will result in an access error. A is wrong because USAGE on database and schema without SELECT on the table does not allow querying. B is wrong because SELECT alone is insufficient without USAGE on the parent database and schema. D is wrong because OWNERSHIP is far more than required — the minimum is USAGE/SELECT, not OWNERSHIP.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Minimum Privileges for Table Access

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants all future tables created in `mydb.public` to automatically have SELECT granted to the `ANALYST` role. Which command achieves this?

options.text: `GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.public TO ROLE analyst;`
`GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.public TO ROLE analyst;`
`ALTER SCHEMA mydb.public SET DEFAULT GRANT SELECT TO ROLE analyst;`
`CREATE POLICY auto_grant AS GRANT SELECT ON NEW TABLES TO ROLE analyst;`

questions.explanation: B is correct because `GRANT ... ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ...` is the Snowflake feature that automatically grants the specified privilege to new tables created in the schema after the grant is issued. This is the future grants mechanism. A is wrong because ON ALL TABLES applies only to existing tables and does not affect future tables. C is wrong because ALTER SCHEMA does not have a DEFAULT GRANT syntax. D is wrong because CREATE POLICY with auto-grant syntax does not exist in Snowflake.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Future Grants

questions.stem: A company implements SAML 2.0 Single Sign-On (SSO) with Snowflake. Which role in Snowflake is responsible for configuring the SAML identity provider settings at the account level?

options.text: SYSADMIN
SECURITYADMIN
ACCOUNTADMIN
PUBLIC

questions.explanation: C is correct because configuring SAML 2.0 SSO at the account level (setting `SAML_IDENTITY_PROVIDER` and `SSO_LOGIN_PAGE`) requires ACCOUNTADMIN privileges, as these are account-level parameters. A is wrong because SYSADMIN manages database objects, not account-level authentication settings. B is wrong because while SECURITYADMIN handles user/role management, configuring the SAML IDP connection at the account level requires ACCOUNTADMIN. D is wrong because PUBLIC has no administrative privileges.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Federated Authentication, SAML SSO Configuration

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following statements are TRUE about Snowflake's Column-Level Security using Dynamic Data Masking? (Select TWO)

options.text: A masking policy can be applied to columns of any data type, including VARIANT.
Only one masking policy can be assigned to a column at a time.
The masking policy function must return the same data type as the column it masks.
Masking policies are evaluated before row access policies when both are applied to the same table.

questions.explanation: B and C are correct. Each column can have at most one masking policy applied at a time. The return data type of the masking policy function must match the data type of the masked column — for example, a policy on a VARCHAR column must return VARCHAR. A is wrong because masking policies support specific data types, and VARIANT columns are not directly supported by standard masking policies (a separate approach is needed for semi-structured data). D is wrong because row access policies are evaluated first, then masking policies are applied to the resulting rows — the order is row access first, then column masking.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Dynamic Data Masking Constraints

questions.stem: A security team wants to audit all login attempts to their Snowflake account. Which Snowflake object or view provides this information?

options.text: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOGIN_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY
ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SESSIONS

questions.explanation: B is correct because `ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY` provides a comprehensive, account-level view of all authentication attempts, including both successful and failed logins, across all users. It retains data for up to 365 days. A is wrong because `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOGIN_HISTORY` is a table function scoped to the current user's session and has a shorter retention period (7 days). C is wrong because QUERY_HISTORY records SQL query execution, not authentication attempts. D is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SESSIONS provides active session information, not login history.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Account Usage, LOGIN_HISTORY View

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to allow a third-party ETL tool to connect using key-pair authentication. Which parameter is set on the Snowflake user object to register the public key?

options.text: ALTER USER etl_user SET PASSWORD = '<public_key>';`
`ALTER USER etl_user SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key_value>';`
`ALTER USER etl_user SET API_KEY = '<public_key_value>';`
`GRANT KEY PAIR TO USER etl_user USING '<public_key_value>';`

questions.explanation: B is correct because key-pair authentication in Snowflake is configured by setting the `RSA_PUBLIC_KEY` parameter on the user object using `ALTER USER ... SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY`. The ETL tool then authenticates using the corresponding private key. A is wrong because PASSWORD is for password-based authentication, not key-pair; setting the public key as a password would not enable key-pair auth. C is wrong because API_KEY is not a valid Snowflake user parameter. D is wrong because GRANT KEY PAIR is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Key-Pair Authentication Setup

questions.stem: A virtual warehouse is consistently showing long query queue times during business hours. Queries are waiting for other queries to finish before executing. What is the MOST likely solution?

options.text: Increase the warehouse size from Medium to Large.
Enable multi-cluster warehouses to handle concurrent queries.
Enable the Search Optimization Service on all queried tables.
Reduce the AUTO_SUSPEND threshold to 30 seconds.

questions.explanation: B is correct because query queuing is a concurrency problem, not a compute-per-query problem. Multi-cluster warehouses address concurrency by adding additional clusters to handle simultaneous queries, eliminating the queue. A is wrong because increasing warehouse size adds more compute per query (better for slow individual queries) but does NOT increase the number of concurrent queries a warehouse can handle. C is wrong because Search Optimization Service improves individual query performance for selective lookups but does not solve queue buildup from concurrency. D is wrong because reducing auto-suspend affects idle credit usage, not query concurrency.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Multi-Cluster Warehouses for Concurrency

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer uses `EXPLAIN USING JSON SELECT * FROM sales WHERE region = 'WEST' AND sale_date = '2024-01-15'`. The output shows `partitionsTotal: 5000` and `partitionsAssigned: 4950`. What does this indicate?

options.text: The query is extremely well-optimized and nearly all partitions are pruned.
Only 50 partitions will be scanned; the remaining 4950 are pruned.
4950 partitions will be scanned out of 5000; very little pruning is occurring.
The query cannot be further optimized because region and sale_date are incompatible filter columns.

questions.explanation: C is correct because `partitionsAssigned` represents the number of micro-partitions that will actually be scanned (cannot be pruned). With 4950 of 5000 partitions assigned, only 50 are being pruned — this is very poor pruning efficiency, likely because the table is not well-clustered on region or sale_date. A is wrong because nearly all partitions being assigned (scanned) is the opposite of well-optimized pruning. B is wrong because partitionsAssigned = partitions TO BE SCANNED (not pruned). D is wrong because the EXPLAIN result indicates a clustering/pruning problem, not query syntax incompatibility.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — EXPLAIN, Partition Pruning Statistics

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following are valid Snowflake materialized view limitations? (Select TWO)

options.text: Materialized views cannot contain window functions (e.g., ROW_NUMBER(), RANK()).
Materialized views can only be created on tables in the same schema.
Materialized views are only available in the Enterprise edition and above.
Materialized views can only be queried by users with the ACCOUNTADMIN role.

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Materialized views in Snowflake do not support window functions such as ROW_NUMBER() or RANK() because these are non-deterministic relative to partition changes and would make incremental maintenance impractical. Materialized views are also an Enterprise edition feature and are not available on the Standard edition. B is wrong because materialized view definitions can join tables across schemas (within the same database), though cross-database joins have limitations. D is wrong because materialized views can be queried by any user with appropriate SELECT privileges — ACCOUNTADMIN is not required.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Materialized View Limitations, Enterprise Edition

questions.stem: A data warehouse team finds that their nightly ETL runs on a Large warehouse take 4 hours. After sizing up to an X-Large warehouse, the same jobs take 2 hours. They then size up to a 2X-Large and see no further improvement. What is the MOST LIKELY explanation?

options.text: The 2X-Large warehouse has a bug that prevents it from using the additional compute nodes.
The ETL jobs are I/O bound — they are bottlenecked by the rate at which data can be read from object storage, not by compute.
Credit consumption limits in the resource monitor are throttling the 2X-Large warehouse.
Snowflake limits ETL jobs to 2 hours maximum regardless of warehouse size.

questions.explanation: B is correct because adding more compute nodes beyond a certain point hits an I/O bottleneck — the rate at which micro-partitions can be read from cloud object storage. If every node is already reading at maximum throughput, adding more nodes does not increase the data delivery rate. This is sometimes called the 'I/O bound' limit. Other bottlenecks like sequential processing logic in stored procedures can also cause this plateau. A is wrong because there is no such bug. C is wrong because resource monitors suspend warehouses but do not silently throttle performance. D is wrong because Snowflake has no 2-hour query duration limit.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Warehouse Sizing Diminishing Returns

questions.stem: A developer creates a `CLUSTER BY (customer_id, order_date)` key on a large orders table. Which query would benefit MOST from this clustering key?

options.text: `SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders` (no WHERE clause)
`SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 999 AND order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'`
`SELECT * FROM orders WHERE amount > 10000`
`SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) FROM orders WHERE region = 'EU'`

questions.explanation: B is correct because the clustering key is on (customer_id, order_date), so a query filtering on both columns can prune the vast majority of micro-partitions — only partitions containing data for customer_id = 999 in January 2024 need to be scanned. A is wrong because a full table scan with no WHERE clause cannot benefit from pruning. C is wrong because `amount` is not in the clustering key, so no pruning on amount is possible. D is wrong because `region` and `product_id` are not in the clustering key, so no micro-partition pruning occurs on those columns.
questions.reference: Snowflake Performance — Clustering Key Query Benefit

questions.stem: A data engineer has already loaded a file `data_2024.csv` from an S3 external stage into a Snowflake table. They run the same COPY INTO command the next day without any changes. What happens by default?

options.text: The file is loaded again, creating duplicate rows.
The file is skipped because Snowflake tracks loaded files and does not reload them by default.
The COPY INTO command fails with an error about a duplicate file.
The file is loaded only if the S3 file timestamp has changed.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake tracks which files have already been loaded via `LOAD_HISTORY` metadata. By default, COPY INTO skips files that have already been successfully loaded to prevent duplicate ingestion. A is wrong because the default behavior is specifically designed to prevent duplicate loading. C is wrong because COPY INTO does not fail with an error for previously loaded files — it simply skips them. D is wrong because Snowflake uses the file metadata hash/path for deduplication, not just the S3 timestamp. The `FORCE = TRUE` option can override this behavior to reload files.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — COPY INTO File Deduplication

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a file format object: `CREATE FILE FORMAT my_json_fmt TYPE = 'JSON' STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE;`. What does STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY do?

options.text: Removes the outermost JSON object keys, keeping only the values.
Treats each element of a top-level JSON array as a separate row during loading, instead of loading the entire array as one VARIANT value.
Converts the JSON array to a CSV-formatted string before loading.
Removes all nested arrays from the JSON before loading to prevent errors.

questions.explanation: B is correct because `STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE` instructs Snowflake to parse a top-level JSON array and insert each array element as a separate row. Without this option, the entire JSON array would be loaded as a single VARIANT row. For example, `[{"id":1},{"id":2}]` would become two rows instead of one. A is wrong because STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY does not affect object keys. C is wrong because the data remains in JSON/VARIANT format — it is not converted to CSV. D is wrong because nested arrays are preserved; only the outermost array wrapper is stripped.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — JSON File Format, STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY

questions.stem: A team wants to query data directly in S3 without loading it into a Snowflake table. Which TWO Snowflake features support querying files directly in cloud storage? (Select TWO)

options.text: External tables
Snowpipe with DIRECT_QUERY = TRUE
Querying files in a stage using SELECT $1, $2 FROM @stage_name
Secure views with EXTERNAL_TABLE = TRUE

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. External tables define a schema over files in an external stage, allowing them to be queried with standard SQL. Snowflake can also directly query staged files using `SELECT $1, $2, ... FROM @stage_name` syntax — the $N notation refers to positional columns in the file. B is wrong because Snowpipe is a continuous ingestion mechanism, not a direct query feature; DIRECT_QUERY is not a valid Snowpipe parameter. D is wrong because secure views with EXTERNAL_TABLE = TRUE is not a valid Snowflake feature syntax.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — External Tables, Querying Staged Files

questions.stem: A developer creates a standard stream on the `orders` table. A downstream task reads and processes the stream. After the task completes and commits, what happens to the rows in the stream?

options.text: The rows remain in the stream indefinitely until manually deleted.
The stream offset advances past the consumed changes; those changes no longer appear in the stream.
The rows are archived in the INFORMATION_SCHEMA for 14 days.
The stream is automatically dropped after it is consumed once.

questions.explanation: B is correct because when a stream is consumed in a DML statement within a committed transaction, the stream's offset advances to the current position. Subsequent reads of the stream will only show changes that occurred after the last consumption point. The data that was consumed is no longer visible in the stream. A is wrong because streams automatically advance their offset upon successful consumption. C is wrong because stream consumption data is not archived to INFORMATION_SCHEMA. D is wrong because streams persist after consumption and continue capturing new changes.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Stream Offset Advancement

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a serverless task using `USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE = 'MEDIUM'`. How is compute billed for this task?

options.text: A Medium virtual warehouse is created, billed at 4 credits/hr for the duration of the task.
Snowflake manages the compute using serverless resources billed per-second based on actual compute used, not warehouse uptime.
The task is free because serverless tasks do not consume credits.
The task must be assigned to an existing warehouse; USER_TASK_MANAGED is only a size hint.

questions.explanation: B is correct because serverless tasks (using USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE instead of WAREHOUSE) use Snowflake-managed compute resources that spin up and down automatically. Billing is based on the actual cloud services compute consumed during task execution, not a full warehouse-hour allocation. The INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE is a hint for initial sizing that Snowflake may adjust based on historical runs. A is wrong because serverless tasks do not create or bill for a traditional virtual warehouse. C is wrong because serverless tasks do consume compute credits, just differently than warehouse-based billing. D is wrong because USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE is the mechanism that opts the task into serverless mode, not a warehouse hint.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Serverless Tasks

questions.stem: Which SQL statement creates a Snowflake table UDF (UDTF) that returns a table of values?

options.text: `CREATE FUNCTION split_values(input VARCHAR) RETURNS TABLE (val VARCHAR) LANGUAGE SQL AS 'SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(input, ","))';`
`CREATE TABLE FUNCTION split_values(input VARCHAR) AS SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(input, ","));`
`CREATE PROCEDURE split_values(input VARCHAR) RETURNS TABLE AS BEGIN SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(input, ",")); END;`
`CREATE VIEW split_values AS SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(:input, ","));`

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake UDTFs (User-Defined Table Functions) use `CREATE FUNCTION ... RETURNS TABLE (column_list) LANGUAGE SQL AS` syntax. This is the correct syntax for creating a table-valued function. B is wrong because CREATE TABLE FUNCTION is not valid Snowflake syntax — it is CREATE FUNCTION with RETURNS TABLE. C is wrong because RETURNS TABLE in a stored procedure context is used for procedure results, and the syntax shown is not the correct UDF pattern. D is wrong because views are static definitions and do not accept parameters.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — User-Defined Table Functions (UDTF)

questions.stem: A developer has a stream `orders_stream` on the `orders` table. The `orders` table has a Time Travel retention period of 1 day. The stream has not been consumed for 3 days. What is the state of the stream?

options.text: The stream is paused and will resume automatically when consumed.
The stream is stale and must be recreated to capture new changes.
The stream only contains the last 24 hours of changes, discarding earlier ones.
The stream is stale but can be reset to the current timestamp using ALTER STREAM.

questions.explanation: B is correct because a stream becomes stale when its offset falls behind the Time Travel retention period of the source table. If the stream was not consumed for 3 days but the table only has 1 day of Time Travel, the stream's offset position is now beyond the available Time Travel window. The stream is stale and the historical change data it was tracking is no longer accessible. The stream must be dropped and recreated to resume CDC. A is wrong because stale streams are not paused — they are in an invalid state that requires recreation. C is wrong because stale streams do not self-trim to the available Time Travel window. D is wrong because there is no ALTER STREAM command to reset a stale stream's offset.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Stale Streams

questions.stem: A team is migrating an Oracle PL/SQL pipeline to Snowflake. Which TWO languages can be used to write stored procedures in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.text: Snowflake Scripting (Snowpark procedural SQL)
PL/SQL (native Oracle compatibility)
Python (using Snowpark for Python)
Go (Golang)

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Snowflake stored procedures support Snowflake Scripting (the native procedural SQL language), Python (via Snowpark), JavaScript, Java, and Scala. B is wrong because Snowflake does not support Oracle PL/SQL natively — Snowflake Scripting is the procedural SQL alternative. D is wrong because Go (Golang) is not a supported language for Snowflake stored procedures as of the current release.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Stored Procedure Languages

questions.stem: A developer creates a sequence and uses it in an INSERT statement: `INSERT INTO orders (id, product) SELECT orders_seq.NEXTVAL, product FROM staging`. What happens if two concurrent sessions insert rows simultaneously using the same sequence?

options.text: Both sessions generate duplicate sequence values, causing primary key violations.
Snowflake guarantees that each session receives unique, non-overlapping sequence values.
One session blocks until the other completes to ensure sequential values.
Sequences are session-scoped, so each session has its own independent counter starting from 1.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake sequences are designed for concurrent use — multiple sessions can call NEXTVAL simultaneously and each will receive unique values without collision. Snowflake guarantees uniqueness but not strict monotonic ordering across concurrent sessions (values may not be sequential, but they will be unique). A is wrong because sequences do not generate duplicate values across concurrent sessions. C is wrong because sequences do not cause blocking — concurrent access is one of their key design properties. D is wrong because sequences are account/schema-scoped objects, not session-scoped.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Sequences, Concurrent Access

questions.stem: A Snowflake account on the Enterprise edition has Time Travel enabled with the maximum 90-day retention. Which TWO Time Travel query syntaxes are valid for accessing historical data? (Select TWO)

options.text: `SELECT * FROM orders AT(OFFSET => -86400);`
`SELECT * FROM orders HISTORY(DAYS => 1);`
`SELECT * FROM orders AT(STATEMENT => '8e5d0ca9-005e-44e6-b858-a8f5b37c5726');`
`SELECT * FROM orders TIMETRAVEL OFFSET -86400;`

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. The `AT(OFFSET => -N)` syntax returns data as of N seconds before the current time. The `AT(STATEMENT => 'query_id')` syntax returns data as of the time immediately after the specified query statement executed. Both are valid Time Travel syntaxes. B is wrong because `HISTORY(DAYS => 1)` is not valid Snowflake SQL syntax. D is wrong because `TIMETRAVEL OFFSET` is not valid Snowflake SQL syntax.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Protection — Time Travel AT Syntax

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider shares a database with two consumer accounts. Consumer A is a paying Snowflake customer. Consumer B is a non-Snowflake customer. How does Consumer B access the shared data?

options.text: Consumer B receives a nightly data export via email in CSV format.
Consumer B must upgrade to a Snowflake account before they can access any shared data.
The provider creates a reader account for Consumer B; the provider pays for the reader account's compute credits.
Consumer B accesses the data via the provider's internal Snowflake account using guest user credentials.

questions.explanation: C is correct because Snowflake provides 'reader accounts' specifically for sharing data with non-Snowflake customers. The data provider creates and manages the reader account; the provider is billed for any compute credits the reader account uses to query the shared data. A is wrong because Snowflake data sharing is live and real-time — data is not exported or emailed. B is wrong because reader accounts are specifically designed so that non-Snowflake customers do not need their own full Snowflake account. D is wrong because guest credentials inside the provider's account are not the mechanism for external sharing.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Protection — Data Sharing, Reader Accounts

questions.stem: Which virtual warehouse size is the SMALLEST available in Snowflake?

options.A: X-Small (XS)
options.B: Small (S)
options.C: Micro
options.D: Nano

questions.explanation: A is correct because X-Small (XS) is the smallest virtual warehouse size in Snowflake, consuming 1 credit per hour. B is wrong because Small is the next size up (2 credits/hr). C and D are wrong because Micro and Nano are not valid Snowflake warehouse sizes.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Virtual Warehouse Sizes and Credit Consumption

questions.stem: A data analyst runs a query and receives results. The exact same query is run again 10 minutes later without any changes to the underlying table. What is the expected behavior?

options.A: The warehouse re-executes the query, consuming compute credits.
options.B: Snowflake returns the cached result instantly from the result cache, consuming no compute credits.
options.C: Snowflake uses the local disk cache on the warehouse nodes to speed up execution.
options.D: The query is blocked until the previous result expires.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake's result cache persists query results for 24 hours. If the identical query is re-executed within that window and the underlying data has not changed, Snowflake serves the result directly from the result cache without engaging the virtual warehouse, consuming zero compute credits. A is wrong because the result cache prevents re-execution. C is wrong because the local disk cache requires the warehouse to be active and does not eliminate compute use. D is wrong because Snowflake does not block repeat queries.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Result Cache, Query Performance

questions.stem: A company needs to run two separate workloads simultaneously: a heavy ETL process and interactive BI queries. Both workloads must not impact each other's performance. What is the BEST Snowflake approach?

options.A: Use a single 4X-Large warehouse shared between both workloads.
options.B: Create two separate virtual warehouses — one for ETL and one for BI queries.
options.C: Enable multi-cluster mode on a single warehouse to split the workloads.
options.D: Schedule the ETL to run after business hours so it does not conflict with BI.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake's architecture allows multiple virtual warehouses to operate independently on the same underlying storage. Creating separate warehouses for ETL and BI provides complete workload isolation — each warehouse has dedicated compute resources and neither can starve the other. A is wrong because a shared warehouse means both workloads compete for the same concurrency slots and memory. C is wrong because multi-cluster is designed to scale one warehouse type for concurrent identical workloads, not to separate different workload types. D is wrong because scheduling avoidance is an operational workaround, not an architectural solution.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Workload Isolation with Multiple Virtual Warehouses

questions.stem: A virtual warehouse is configured with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 3 and MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 using the Standard scaling policy. The warehouse currently has 2 active clusters. The load drops significantly and there is no queuing. What happens?

options.A: The warehouse immediately scales down to 1 cluster.
options.B: The warehouse waits for 2-3 consecutive checks showing low load before scaling down a cluster.
options.C: The warehouse scales down only when manually resized by an administrator.
options.D: The warehouse scales down to 0 clusters, suspending entirely.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake uses a conservative scale-down approach for multi-cluster warehouses. Even with the Standard policy, Snowflake performs consecutive load checks and only removes a cluster after confirming that reduced capacity is sustained — typically after 2-3 consecutive 1-minute checks show the cluster is underutilized. This prevents thrashing from brief load dips. A is wrong because scale-down is not instantaneous. C is wrong because scale-down is automatic for multi-cluster warehouses. D is wrong because MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 prevents the warehouse from scaling below 1 cluster.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Multi-Cluster Warehouse Auto-Scaling Behavior

questions.stem: A user wants to run a query that would normally take 10 minutes on a Medium warehouse. They need it to complete in about 5 minutes. Assuming the query benefits from additional compute (not I/O bound), what should they do?

options.A: Enable the query result cache.
options.B: Resize the warehouse to a Large size before running the query.
options.C: Enable multi-cluster warehouses.
options.D: Reduce the AUTO_SUSPEND to 30 seconds.

questions.explanation: B is correct because increasing the warehouse size (vertical scaling) roughly doubles the compute resources, which can approximately halve the runtime for compute-bound queries. A Large warehouse has twice the compute nodes of a Medium warehouse. A is wrong because the result cache only helps if the query was previously run with no underlying data changes. C is wrong because multi-cluster warehouses add additional clusters for concurrency (more users/queries simultaneously), not to speed up a single query. D is wrong because AUTO_SUSPEND affects idle billing, not query execution speed.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Virtual Warehouse Sizing and Query Performance

questions.stem: A Snowflake resource monitor is configured with CREDIT_QUOTA = 100 and two actions: NOTIFY at 75% and SUSPEND at 100%. Which TWO statements are TRUE about this resource monitor? (Select TWO)

options.A: When the warehouse reaches 75 credits consumed, it automatically suspends until the quota resets.
options.B: When the warehouse reaches 75 credits consumed, Snowflake sends an alert notification but continues running.
options.C: When the warehouse reaches 100 credits consumed, it suspends but allows currently running queries to complete.
options.D: When the warehouse reaches 100 credits consumed, it immediately kills all running queries.

questions.explanation: B and C are correct. The NOTIFY action at 75% sends an email notification to account administrators but does NOT suspend the warehouse — execution continues. The SUSPEND action at 100% (SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE would kill queries immediately) gracefully suspends the warehouse after currently running queries complete, then prevents new queries from starting. A is wrong because the 75% action is NOTIFY, not SUSPEND. D is wrong because SUSPEND (not SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE) allows in-flight queries to finish before suspending.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Resource Monitors, SUSPEND vs SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE

questions.stem: The Query Acceleration Service (QAS) is enabled on a warehouse. Which type of query benefits MOST from QAS?

options.A: Short, highly selective point-lookup queries that read a single row.
options.B: Large scans with outlier partitions — queries where a small number of partitions contain disproportionately more data than average.
options.C: Queries that are waiting in the concurrency queue due to high user load.
options.D: JOIN-heavy queries between large tables with no filter predicates.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Query Acceleration Service specifically targets queries with 'outlier' micro-partitions — situations where a few partitions are much larger than average, causing the query to spend most of its time processing those few partitions. QAS offloads that outlier work to Snowflake-managed serverless compute, preventing the outlier partitions from bottlenecking the overall query. A is wrong because point lookups are already fast due to pruning and don't benefit from parallelism. C is wrong because QAS does not address concurrency queuing — multi-cluster warehouses handle that. D is wrong because full table joins without filters rarely have outlier partition patterns that QAS can exploit.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Query Acceleration Service

questions.stem: A warehouse is resized from Medium to Large WHILE queries are actively running on it. What happens to the in-flight queries?

options.A: In-flight queries are immediately killed and must be restarted on the new Large warehouse.
options.B: In-flight queries continue running on the existing Medium-sized compute; the resize applies to subsequently queued or submitted queries.
options.C: The resize is rejected until all running queries complete.
options.D: The warehouse splits: existing queries finish on Medium nodes while new queries start on Large nodes simultaneously.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake applies warehouse resizes gracefully. Queries that are already running continue on the existing cluster configuration. The new size takes effect for subsequent queries. This means a resize mid-execution does not disrupt running work. A is wrong because Snowflake does not kill in-flight queries on resize. C is wrong because resizes are not blocked by active queries. D is wrong because the warehouse does not split into concurrent Medium and Large clusters during a resize.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Resizing a Virtual Warehouse

questions.stem: What is the default Time Travel retention period for a newly created table in a Snowflake Standard edition account?

options.A: 0 days (disabled by default)
options.B: 1 day
options.C: 7 days
options.D: 90 days

questions.explanation: B is correct because the default Time Travel retention period is 1 day (24 hours) for all Snowflake editions. In Standard edition, 1 day is also the maximum. In Enterprise edition, the retention can be extended up to 90 days. A is wrong because Time Travel is enabled by default, not disabled. C is wrong because 7 days is the Fail-Safe window, not the default Time Travel period. D is wrong because 90 days is only available on Enterprise edition and above, and is not the default.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Time Travel Retention Periods

questions.stem: A developer creates a zero-copy clone of a 1 TB production table. Initially, how much additional storage does the clone consume?

options.A: 1 TB — a full copy of all micro-partitions is made.
options.B: 0 bytes — the clone shares all micro-partitions with the original until data is modified.
options.C: 500 GB — Snowflake stores a compressed delta of the original data.
options.D: Depends on the size of the virtual warehouse used for cloning.

questions.explanation: B is correct because zero-copy cloning in Snowflake does not copy any data at creation time. The clone is a metadata operation — the clone's metadata points to the same underlying micro-partitions as the original table. Additional storage is only incurred when either the original or the clone has data modified, causing Snowflake to write new micro-partitions for the changed data (copy-on-write). A is wrong because zero-copy clone creates no physical data copy at clone time. C is wrong because no delta compression or partial copy is stored. D is wrong because cloning is a metadata operation that does not use a virtual warehouse.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Zero-Copy Cloning

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes Snowflake's micro-partitioning?

options.A: Snowflake requires users to manually define partition boundaries using ALTER TABLE ... ADD PARTITION.
options.B: Snowflake automatically divides table data into contiguous units (50–500 MB compressed), each stored with metadata about min/max values per column.
options.C: Micro-partitions are equivalent to traditional database pages, each exactly 8 KB in size.
options.D: Micro-partitions store data in row-oriented format to optimize point-lookup queries.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake automatically creates and manages micro-partitions as contiguous units of data, typically 50–500 MB of uncompressed data. Each micro-partition stores column metadata (min, max, distinct count, null count) that enables efficient partition pruning during query execution. A is wrong because micro-partitioning is fully automatic — users cannot manually define partition boundaries. C is wrong because micro-partitions are much larger than 8 KB database pages. D is wrong because Snowflake stores data in a columnar format within each micro-partition, which optimizes analytical queries.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Micro-Partitioning and Data Clustering

questions.stem: A Snowflake table has a CLUSTER BY (region, sale_year) key. After heavy DML (many INSERTs and DELETEs), the Average Depth (AD) of the table rises to 8 and the Average Overlap Depth (AOD) is also very high. What does this indicate and what should be done?

options.A: The table is optimally clustered; high Average Depth means data is well-consolidated.
options.B: The table has become stale/poorly clustered; a RECLUSTER operation should be triggered (automatic clustering will handle it if enabled).
options.C: High Average Depth means the table has too many columns; the clustering key should be removed.
options.D: The table needs to be vacuumed using VACUUM TABLE to compact micro-partitions.

questions.explanation: B is correct because high Average Depth and high Average Overlap Depth (AOD) indicate that the table's data is no longer well-ordered relative to the clustering key — many micro-partitions overlap in the key column ranges. This means partition pruning is ineffective for queries on those columns. If Automatic Clustering is enabled, Snowflake will eventually recluster the table in the background. If not, a manual RECLUSTER operation can be triggered. A is wrong because high AD/AOD indicates poor clustering, not good clustering. C is wrong because Average Depth is a clustering metric, not related to column count. D is wrong because VACUUM TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command — Snowflake uses RECLUSTER for clustering maintenance.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Clustering Information Functions, RECLUSTER

questions.stem: Which TWO objects can be zero-copy cloned in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.A: Tables (including their data)
options.B: Databases (creating a clone of the entire database with all its schemas and tables)
options.C: Virtual warehouses
options.D: Network policies

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. Snowflake supports zero-copy cloning of tables, schemas, and databases. Cloning a database clones all its contained schemas and tables in a single metadata operation. C is wrong because virtual warehouses are compute resources and cannot be cloned — they are created independently. D is wrong because network policies are account-level configuration objects and do not support cloning.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Zero-Copy Cloning — Supported Objects

questions.stem: A developer runs `CREATE TABLE orders_backup CLONE orders AT(OFFSET => -3600)`. What does this create?

options.A: A backup table containing only the rows that changed in the last 3600 seconds.
options.B: A zero-copy clone of the orders table as it existed exactly 3600 seconds (1 hour) ago.
options.C: A scheduled backup job that runs every 3600 seconds.
options.D: This is invalid syntax — CLONE does not support AT() time travel.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake allows zero-copy clones to be created from a historical Time Travel point using AT() or BEFORE() syntax. The resulting table reflects the state of the source table at the specified offset (3600 seconds = 1 hour ago). This is useful for creating point-in-time snapshots. A is wrong because the clone contains the complete table state at that historical point, not just changed rows. C is wrong because this is a one-time DDL statement, not a scheduled job. D is wrong because CLONE with AT() is valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Cloning Considerations — Using Time Travel with CLONE

questions.stem: A table's Time Travel retention period is set to 7 days. After 7 days, the data enters Fail-Safe. How long does Fail-Safe last, and who can recover data from it?

options.A: Fail-Safe lasts 24 hours and can be recovered by the ACCOUNTADMIN role using UNDROP TABLE.
options.B: Fail-Safe lasts 7 days and can only be recovered by Snowflake Support — it is not accessible via SQL.
options.C: Fail-Safe lasts 90 days and can be queried using the AT(FAIL_SAFE => TRUE) syntax.
options.D: Fail-Safe lasts indefinitely and is automatically converted to a long-term backup archive.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Fail-Safe provides an additional 7-day retention period after Time Travel expires, but it is exclusively managed by Snowflake Support. Users cannot access Fail-Safe data via SQL commands — it is a last-resort disaster recovery mechanism for catastrophic failures. A is wrong because Fail-Safe lasts 7 days (not 24 hours) and is not accessible via UNDROP TABLE. C is wrong because AT(FAIL_SAFE => TRUE) is not valid Snowflake syntax. D is wrong because Fail-Safe has a fixed 7-day window and is not converted to long-term archives.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Fail-Safe Storage

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to load a CSV file from their local laptop into a Snowflake internal stage. Which command uploads the file from local disk to the stage?

options.A: COPY INTO @my_stage FROM '/local/path/data.csv'
options.B: PUT file:///local/path/data.csv @my_stage
options.C: UPLOAD '/local/path/data.csv' TO @my_stage
options.D: INSERT INTO @my_stage SELECT * FROM '/local/path/data.csv'

questions.explanation: B is correct because the PUT command is used to upload files from a local file system to a Snowflake internal stage. The syntax is `PUT file://<local_path> @<stage_name>`. PUT is executed from the SnowSQL CLI or Snowflake drivers. A is wrong because COPY INTO is used to load data from a stage into a table, not to upload files to a stage. C is wrong because UPLOAD is not a valid Snowflake command. D is wrong because INSERT INTO is for inserting row data, not file uploading.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: PUT Command — Uploading Files to Internal Stages

questions.stem: A data engineer runs: `COPY INTO sales FROM @my_stage/data.csv.gz ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE'`. The file contains 1000 rows and 50 rows fail validation. What is the result?

options.A: The entire load is rejected and no rows are inserted due to the 50 errors.
options.B: 950 valid rows are loaded; the 50 invalid rows are skipped and errors are recorded in the copy history.
options.C: Snowflake loads all 1000 rows and marks the 50 invalid ones with NULL values.
options.D: The command fails because ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE' is not a valid COPY INTO option.

questions.explanation: B is correct because `ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE'` instructs Snowflake to skip rows that fail to parse or validate and continue processing the remaining rows. The 950 valid rows are inserted into the table. Details about the 50 failed rows are available in the COPY_HISTORY or load history. A is wrong because ON_ERROR = 'ABORT_STATEMENT' (the default) would reject the entire load, but CONTINUE overrides this. C is wrong because Snowflake does not insert malformed rows with NULLs under CONTINUE — it skips them. D is wrong because ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE' is a valid and commonly used COPY INTO option.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: COPY INTO — ON_ERROR Options

questions.stem: What is the purpose of Snowpipe in Snowflake?

options.A: To provide a SQL interface for piping data between Snowflake tables in real time.
options.B: To enable continuous, near-real-time automated data ingestion from a stage into a Snowflake table.
options.C: To establish a network pipe (VPN) between an on-premises data center and Snowflake.
options.D: To schedule batch COPY INTO operations at defined time intervals.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowpipe is Snowflake's continuous data ingestion service. It monitors a stage (typically triggered by cloud storage notifications like S3 event notifications or SQS messages) and automatically loads new files as they arrive, providing near-real-time ingestion without managing scheduled batch jobs. A is wrong because Snowpipe does not pipe data between Snowflake tables — that is done with INSERT/MERGE or streams/tasks. C is wrong because Snowpipe is a data ingestion service, not a network pipe. D is wrong because Snowpipe is event-driven, not time-interval scheduled — scheduled batch loads use task-triggered COPY INTO.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Snowpipe — Continuous Data Ingestion

questions.stem: A team is loading a large Parquet file from S3. Which TWO COPY INTO options are specific to or particularly relevant for loading Parquet files? (Select TWO)

options.A: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE
options.B: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE
options.C: USE_LOGICAL_TYPE = TRUE
options.D: FIELD_DELIMITER = ','

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE is used when loading Parquet (or other columnar formats) to map source file columns to target table columns by name rather than by position, which is critical for Parquet's named schema. USE_LOGICAL_TYPE = TRUE instructs Snowflake to respect Parquet logical types (e.g., DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP) and convert them to the appropriate Snowflake types rather than loading raw physical values. B is wrong because STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY applies to JSON files that contain a top-level array. D is wrong because FIELD_DELIMITER is a CSV-specific option — Parquet is a columnar binary format with no field delimiters.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: COPY INTO — Parquet File Format Options

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to unload query results from Snowflake to an S3 bucket in CSV format with gzip compression. Which command achieves this?

options.A: EXPORT TABLE sales TO 's3://mybucket/exports/' FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV) COMPRESSION = GZIP;
options.B: COPY INTO 's3://mybucket/exports/' FROM (SELECT * FROM sales) FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV COMPRESSION = GZIP);
options.C: UNLOAD SELECT * FROM sales TO 's3://mybucket/exports/' FORMAT CSV GZIP;
options.D: PUT (SELECT * FROM sales) TO 's3://mybucket/exports/' COMPRESSION = GZIP;

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake uses COPY INTO to unload data from a table or query into an external stage or cloud storage location. The syntax `COPY INTO '<external_location>' FROM (SELECT ...)` with FILE_FORMAT options specifying TYPE = CSV and COMPRESSION = GZIP is the correct unloading syntax. A is wrong because EXPORT TABLE is not valid Snowflake SQL. C is wrong because UNLOAD is not a Snowflake command (it exists in other systems like Redshift). D is wrong because PUT is for uploading local files to internal stages, not for exporting query results.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: COPY INTO (Location) — Unloading Data

questions.stem: What is the difference between a named internal stage and a table stage in Snowflake?

options.A: Table stages are permanent and shared across multiple tables; named stages are temporary and session-specific.
options.B: A named internal stage is a separate Snowflake object that can be used to load data into any table; a table stage is automatically created with each table and can only be used to load that specific table.
options.C: Named internal stages support only CSV files; table stages support all file formats.
options.D: Table stages require ACCOUNTADMIN privileges to use; named stages can be used by any role.

questions.explanation: B is correct because every Snowflake table has an automatically created table stage (@%table_name) that is tied to that specific table and can only load into it. Named internal stages are explicit Snowflake objects (created with CREATE STAGE) that can be used to load data into any table. Named stages offer more flexibility and are preferred for production pipelines. A is wrong because table stages persist as long as the table exists and named stages are not session-specific. C is wrong because both stage types support multiple file formats. D is wrong because table stages do not require ACCOUNTADMIN.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Staging Data Files — Stage Types

questions.stem: Which system-defined Snowflake role is responsible for managing user and role creation across the account?

options.A: SYSADMIN
options.B: SECURITYADMIN
options.C: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.D: USERADMIN

questions.explanation: D is correct because USERADMIN is the system-defined role in Snowflake specifically designed for managing users and roles. It has CREATE USER and CREATE ROLE privileges at the account level. B is wrong because SECURITYADMIN manages security policies and can also manage users/roles but is a higher-level role. A is wrong because SYSADMIN manages databases and warehouse objects, not users/roles by default. C is wrong because ACCOUNTADMIN is the highest-privilege role that encompasses all capabilities but is not the designated user management role for daily operations.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: System-Defined Roles — USERADMIN

questions.stem: A Snowflake network policy is created with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('192.168.1.0/24') and BLOCKED_IP_LIST = ('192.168.1.100'). A user attempts to connect from IP 192.168.1.100. What happens?

options.A: The connection is allowed because 192.168.1.100 is within the allowed /24 subnet.
options.B: The connection is blocked because the BLOCKED_IP_LIST takes precedence over ALLOWED_IP_LIST.
options.C: The network policy is invalid — you cannot have both ALLOWED and BLOCKED lists.
options.D: The connection is allowed but logged as a policy violation.

questions.explanation: B is correct because when both ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST are defined, the BLOCKED_IP_LIST takes precedence. Even though 192.168.1.100 falls within the allowed subnet range, the explicit block for that specific IP wins. A is wrong because the subnet match does not override the explicit block. C is wrong because Snowflake supports both allowed and blocked IP lists in the same network policy. D is wrong because Snowflake blocks the connection and does not allow it with just a log entry.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Network Policies — IP Allowlist and Blocklist

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature allows an administrator to define a function that returns a boolean condition to restrict which rows a given role can see in a table?

options.A: Dynamic Data Masking
options.B: Row Access Policy
options.C: Secure View
options.D: Column-Level Security

questions.explanation: B is correct because a Row Access Policy in Snowflake is a schema-level object containing a SQL function that returns TRUE or FALSE to determine whether a specific row is visible to the querying user or role. When attached to a table, the policy filters rows transparently for each query. A is wrong because Dynamic Data Masking hides or obfuscates column values but does not filter rows. C is wrong because a secure view restricts the query definition but is not a policy applied at the row level dynamically based on role. D is wrong because Column-Level Security (masking) affects column values, not row visibility.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Row Access Policies

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake's SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) integration are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.A: SCIM allows an identity provider (e.g., Okta, Azure AD) to automatically provision and deprovision Snowflake users and roles.
options.B: SCIM eliminates the need for Snowflake passwords entirely by replacing them with OAuth tokens.
options.C: SCIM user provisioning requires the SCIM_PROVISIONER system role in Snowflake.
options.D: SCIM can synchronize group memberships from the identity provider to Snowflake role assignments.

questions.explanation: A and D are correct. SCIM is an open standard that allows identity providers like Okta or Azure AD to automatically create, update, and deactivate Snowflake user accounts and synchronize group memberships to Snowflake role assignments. This enables centralized identity lifecycle management. B is wrong because SCIM handles user provisioning/deprovisioning, not authentication — it does not eliminate passwords; SSO/SAML handles authentication separately. C is wrong because SCIM provisioning uses an OAuth token scoped to a specific security integration, not a SCIM_PROVISIONER system role (which does not exist as a system-defined role).
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: SCIM Provisioning

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a masking policy: `CREATE MASKING POLICY email_mask AS (val STRING) RETURNS STRING -> CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() IN ('ANALYST') THEN val ELSE '***MASKED***' END;`. The policy is applied to the `email` column in the `customers` table. A user using the PUBLIC role queries the table. What does the `email` column return?

options.A: The full email address, because PUBLIC is a parent of all roles.
options.B: '***MASKED***' because the user's current role is PUBLIC, not ANALYST.
options.C: NULL because masking policies return NULL for unauthorized roles.
options.D: An error because masking policies require the user to have ANALYST role granted.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the masking policy evaluates CURRENT_ROLE() at query time. The user is operating under the PUBLIC role, which is not in the ('ANALYST') list, so the ELSE branch returns '***MASKED***'. A is wrong because the policy checks the CURRENT_ROLE(), not role hierarchy — the PUBLIC role being a parent does not satisfy the ANALYST check. C is wrong because the masking policy explicitly returns '***MASKED***' (a string), not NULL, for non-ANALYST roles. D is wrong because masking policies do not error on unauthorized roles — they return the masked value.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Dynamic Data Masking Policy Logic

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature provides an immutable, tamper-evident log of all SQL operations, login events, and data access across an account?

options.A: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
options.B: The Snowflake Data Exchange audit trail
options.C: Snowflake Access History (ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY)
options.D: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS

questions.explanation: C is correct because ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY provides detailed records of all data access events — including which queries accessed which base table columns and rows, by which user, at what time. This view is designed for data governance, compliance auditing, and sensitivity classification tracking. A is wrong because QUERY_HISTORY shows query text and execution metadata but does not detail which specific columns or rows were accessed. B is wrong because the Data Exchange is a data sharing marketplace, not an audit log. D is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS shows column metadata definitions, not access audit logs.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Access History — Column-Level Lineage

questions.stem: What Snowflake data type is used to store semi-structured data such as JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, or XML?

options.A: TEXT
options.B: BLOB
options.C: VARIANT
options.D: STRUCT

questions.explanation: C is correct because VARIANT is Snowflake's native data type for storing semi-structured data. A VARIANT column can hold any JSON, XML, Avro, ORC, or Parquet document up to 16 MB in size. Snowflake automatically parses and stores it in an optimized internal format. A is wrong because TEXT (or VARCHAR) stores plain text strings without structured parsing. B is wrong because BLOB is not a Snowflake native type for semi-structured data. D is wrong because STRUCT is not a Snowflake data type — though OBJECT is related.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: VARIANT Data Type

questions.stem: A VARIANT column `data` contains `{"user": {"id": 101, "name": "Alice"}}`. Which expression correctly extracts the value of `name` as a string?

options.A: data.user.name
options.B: data['user']['name']::STRING
options.C: GET(data, 'user.name')::STRING
options.D: PARSE_JSON(data):user:name::STRING

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake supports bracket notation for navigating nested VARIANT structures. `data['user']['name']` extracts the nested value, and `::STRING` casts it to a VARCHAR. D is also close to valid (colon notation), but PARSE_JSON is redundant on a VARIANT column that is already parsed. A is wrong because dot notation (data.user.name) is not valid in Snowflake SQL for VARIANT navigation — you must use colon (:) or bracket ([]) notation. C is wrong because GET() only navigates one level at a time using exact keys and does not support dotted path strings.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Querying Semi-Structured Data — Dot and Bracket Notation

questions.stem: A table has a VARIANT column `tags` that contains JSON arrays like `["sales","vip","premium"]`. A developer wants to query each tag as a separate row. Which Snowflake function should be used?

options.A: SPLIT(tags, ',')
options.B: UNNEST(tags)
options.C: FLATTEN(INPUT => tags)
options.D: ARRAY_TO_ROWS(tags)

questions.explanation: C is correct because FLATTEN is the Snowflake table function used to expand VARIANT arrays (and nested structures) into multiple rows. Used with LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => tags), each element of the JSON array becomes a separate row with columns like VALUE, INDEX, and KEY. A is wrong because SPLIT works on string values and returns an ARRAY — it does not create rows. B is wrong because UNNEST is a PostgreSQL/BigQuery function not supported in Snowflake. D is wrong because ARRAY_TO_ROWS is not a valid Snowflake function.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: FLATTEN — Lateral Flatten of VARIANT Arrays

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake's schema detection for semi-structured data are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.A: INFER_SCHEMA can automatically detect column names and types from staged Parquet, ORC, Avro, and JSON files.
options.B: Schema detection using INFER_SCHEMA works only for CSV files.
options.C: GENERATE_COLUMN_LIST returns a column definition string from INFER_SCHEMA output that can be used in CREATE TABLE statements.
options.D: INFER_SCHEMA requires the files to be loaded into a Snowflake table before the schema can be detected.

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. INFER_SCHEMA is a Snowflake table function that reads staged files (Parquet, ORC, Avro, JSON with STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY) and returns the inferred column names and data types without loading the data into a table first. GENERATE_COLUMN_LIST is a helper function that formats the INFER_SCHEMA output into a usable column definition string for CREATE TABLE or COPY INTO statements. B is wrong because INFER_SCHEMA does not work with CSV — CSV files do not carry embedded schema information. D is wrong because INFER_SCHEMA reads files directly from the stage, before any table load.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: INFER_SCHEMA, Schema Detection

questions.stem: A developer uses `TRY_CAST(data:price::STRING AS NUMBER)` on a VARIANT column where some rows have `price` as a string like `"N/A"`. What is returned for those rows?

options.A: An error is raised for each row where the cast fails.
options.B: NULL is returned for rows where the cast to NUMBER fails.
options.C: 0 is returned as a default numeric value for failed casts.
options.D: The original string value 'N/A' is returned unchanged.

questions.explanation: B is correct because TRY_CAST is the safe casting function in Snowflake that returns NULL instead of raising an error when a type conversion fails. For rows where `data:price` is 'N/A' (which cannot be converted to a NUMBER), TRY_CAST returns NULL. A is wrong because TRY_CAST specifically avoids raising errors — that is the purpose of the TRY_ prefix (use CAST or :: for error-raising behavior). C is wrong because TRY_CAST returns NULL, not 0, on failure. D is wrong because TRY_CAST always returns the target type or NULL — it never returns the original value in the original type.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: TRY_CAST Function

questions.stem: A developer notices that a query runs in 10 seconds the first time but only 1 second on the second run with no data changes. The virtual warehouse remained active between runs. Which performance feature is MOST likely responsible for the speedup?

options.A: Automatic Query Optimization (AQE) rewriting the query on the second run.
options.B: The local disk cache (data cache) on the warehouse nodes retaining previously read micro-partitions in memory/SSD.
options.C: The result cache returning the precomputed answer.
options.D: Search Optimization Service pre-indexing the table between the two runs.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the virtual warehouse's local disk cache (also called the data cache or remote disk cache) stores micro-partitions that were read from cloud storage during the first query. On the second run, the warehouse reads the same partitions from local SSD cache instead of fetching them from remote object storage, which is significantly faster. The result cache would return results in sub-millisecond time (essentially instant), not 1 second, so 1 second suggests compute still ran but faster due to cached data. A is wrong because AQE performs automatic plan adjustment, not a separate optimization between runs. D is wrong because Search Optimization Service works on selective point-lookup patterns, not general scan speed.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Data Cache (Local Disk Cache)

questions.stem: When examining a query profile in Snowflake, a developer sees that the `TableScan` node shows `Bytes scanned: 500 GB` but `Bytes spilled to local storage: 200 GB`. What does 'bytes spilled to local storage' indicate?

options.A: 200 GB of query results were written to an internal Snowflake table as overflow.
options.B: The virtual warehouse ran out of in-memory space during query execution and wrote intermediate data to local SSD, degrading performance.
options.C: Snowflake moved 200 GB of data to the result cache for future query reuse.
options.D: The query read 200 GB of redundant data that was pruned after the initial scan.

questions.explanation: B is correct because 'spilled to local storage' in the Snowflake query profile means that the warehouse's in-memory capacity was insufficient to hold all intermediate query data (e.g., hash tables for joins or sort buffers), causing Snowflake to spill overflow to the warehouse nodes' local SSD. This is significantly slower than in-memory operations and indicates the warehouse may be undersized for the query's memory requirements. A is wrong because spilling does not write to user tables. C is wrong because spilling is not related to the result cache. D is wrong because pruned data is not scanned at all — spilling refers to intermediate data written mid-execution.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Query Profile — Spilling to Local and Remote Storage

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to improve query performance for highly selective queries (e.g., looking up a specific customer_id in a 10-billion-row table). Which TWO Snowflake features are BEST suited for this use case? (Select TWO)

options.A: Search Optimization Service
options.B: Materialized Views
options.C: CLUSTER BY (customer_id)
options.D: Increasing the warehouse size to 4X-Large

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. The Search Optimization Service creates a persistent search access path for equality and range predicates, dramatically speeding up highly selective point lookups on columns that don't have good natural clustering. CLUSTER BY (customer_id) physically organizes micro-partitions by customer_id, enabling partition pruning to scan only the handful of partitions containing the target customer. Both approaches reduce the amount of data scanned. B is wrong because materialized views pre-aggregate data and are best for analytical queries, not row-level lookups. D is wrong because a larger warehouse scans data faster but still scans all 10 billion rows — it does not reduce the amount of data read.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Search Optimization Service, Clustering Keys for Selective Queries

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider creates a share and adds a table to it. The consumer mounts the share and queries the table. When does the consumer see new data inserted by the provider into the shared table?

options.A: Only after the provider manually refreshes the share.
options.B: Immediately — Snowflake data sharing is live and the consumer always queries the provider's current data.
options.C: After a 24-hour synchronization window.
options.D: Only when the consumer recreates their imported database from the share.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake data sharing uses a zero-copy, live model. The consumer's imported database is a logical view over the provider's storage — no data is physically copied. Any changes the provider makes (INSERTs, UPDATEs, DELETEs) are immediately visible to the consumer in their next query. A is wrong because there is no manual refresh mechanism — data sharing is always live. C is wrong because there is no synchronization delay. D is wrong because the imported database does not need to be recreated.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Data Sharing — Live, Zero-Copy Architecture

questions.stem: Which object types can a Snowflake data provider add to a share? (Choose the BEST answer)

options.A: Only tables
options.B: Tables, secure views, and secure UDFs
options.C: Tables, regular views, and stored procedures
options.D: Only secure views (tables cannot be shared directly)

questions.explanation: B is correct because a Snowflake share can include tables, secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs. Secure views are often used to share a subset of a table or join result while hiding the underlying table definition. A is wrong because secure views and secure UDFs can also be added to shares. C is wrong because regular (non-secure) views cannot be added to shares — only secure views — and stored procedures cannot be shared directly. D is wrong because tables can be shared directly.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Data Sharing — Objects That Can Be Shared

questions.stem: A data provider wants to share a view of customer data that should hide the underlying SQL logic (table joins and business rules) from the consumer. Which Snowflake object should be shared?

options.A: A regular view with GRANT SELECT on the view.
options.B: A secure view created with CREATE SECURE VIEW.
options.C: A materialized view created with CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW.
options.D: A stored procedure that returns a result set.

questions.explanation: B is correct because a SECURE VIEW in Snowflake hides the view's definition (SQL logic) from the consumer. When a view is marked as SECURE, the SHOW VIEWS and GET_DDL() output does not expose the view body to non-owner roles. Only secure views can be added to data shares. A is wrong because regular views can be shared only in limited contexts, and they do not hide the view definition. C is wrong because while materialized views can be made secure, standard materialized views are not automatically secure and the question asks about hiding SQL logic — secure view is the direct answer. D is wrong because stored procedures cannot be added to shares.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Secure Views in Data Sharing

questions.stem: A data provider is on AWS us-east-1. A consumer account is on AWS eu-west-1. The provider creates a standard share. Can the consumer access the data?

options.A: Yes — Snowflake data sharing works across any cloud regions without restriction.
options.B: No — standard shares only work within the same account.
options.C: Data sharing across regions requires a Replication Group or Cross-Cloud Auto-Fulfillment; a simple share to a different region/cloud is limited.
options.D: Yes, but the consumer must pay additional inter-region data transfer fees charged by Snowflake.

questions.explanation: C is correct because a basic Snowflake share works natively only within the same cloud region. To share data across regions or cloud providers, the provider must either use Database Replication to replicate data to the consumer's region and then share from the replica, or use the Snowflake Marketplace's Cross-Cloud Auto-Fulfillment feature which handles replication automatically. A is wrong because standard shares are region-scoped. B is wrong because shares work across accounts within the same region, not just within the same account. D is wrong because Snowflake does not directly charge consumers inter-region data transfer fees at the share level.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Data Sharing Across Regions and Cloud Platforms

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake reader accounts are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.A: Reader accounts are created and managed by the data provider.
options.B: Reader accounts can sign up for their own full Snowflake account at no extra cost.
options.C: The data provider is billed for all compute credits consumed by reader accounts.
