options.D: Reader accounts can share data further to other accounts.

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Reader accounts are provisioned by the data provider using the CREATE MANAGED ACCOUNT command. The provider has full control over creating, managing, and dropping reader accounts. Any compute credits used by the reader account to query shared data are billed to the provider's Snowflake account. B is wrong because reader accounts are managed, restricted accounts — they are not upgradeable to full Snowflake accounts within the same provision. D is wrong because reader accounts cannot re-share data — they can only query the data shared to them by the provider.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Reader Accounts — Provider Billing and Management

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Data Marketplace?

options.A: An internal Snowflake tool for managing data warehouse assets and schemas.
options.B: A curated online catalog where providers publish live data products that consumers can discover and access directly in their Snowflake accounts.
options.C: A third-party app store for purchasing Snowflake compute credits at discounted rates.
options.D: A file transfer service for sharing large datasets between Snowflake accounts.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Snowflake Marketplace (formerly Data Marketplace) is a data commerce platform where third-party data providers publish live, ready-to-query datasets. Consumers can discover, request access to, and instantly mount these datasets into their own Snowflake account without any ETL or data movement — the data is shared live using Snowflake's native sharing architecture. A is wrong because the Marketplace is an external data commerce platform, not an internal schema management tool. C is wrong because it has nothing to do with credit purchasing. D is wrong because no files are transferred — data sharing is live and zero-copy.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Snowflake Marketplace

questions.stem: A Snowflake provider wants to offer a dataset commercially with row-level usage metering (charging consumers per row they query). Which feature enables this?

options.A: Standard data shares with a custom billing report.
options.B: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms.
options.C: Snowflake Marketplace Listings with Data Products and monetization enabled.
options.D: Reader accounts with a fixed monthly fee applied by ACCOUNTADMIN.

questions.explanation: C is correct because the Snowflake Marketplace supports monetized listings where providers can charge consumers for access to their data. Snowflake handles the billing and revenue split. Row-level or query-based metering is supported through the Snowflake Native App framework and monetization features in Marketplace listings. A is wrong because standard shares have no built-in billing or metering mechanism. B is wrong because Data Clean Rooms are for privacy-preserving collaborative analytics, not commercial data monetization. D is wrong because reader accounts bill the provider, not the consumer.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Monetizing Data on the Snowflake Marketplace

questions.stem: A company has a Snowflake account with sensitive PII data. They want to allow a partner to run aggregate analytics on this data without either party seeing the other's raw data. Which Snowflake feature is designed for this use case?

options.A: Zero-copy clone shared between the two accounts.
options.B: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms.
options.C: A secure view shared via a standard data share.
options.D: Cross-account database replication with row-level masking.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake Data Clean Rooms are purpose-built for privacy-preserving collaboration. They allow multiple parties to contribute data and run predefined analyses without either party directly accessing the other's raw data. Aggregate results are returned, but PII is never exposed across the boundary. A is wrong because a zero-copy clone creates a copy accessible to the other party, exposing raw data. C is wrong because a secure view still shares the underlying data directly — the consumer can see all rows the view exposes. D is wrong because replication + masking still makes the replicated data available, just with column masking.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms

questions.stem: A data consumer has mounted a shared database from a provider. Which role in the CONSUMER account is required to CREATE DATABASE from a share?

options.A: SYSADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN
options.B: Any role with IMPORT SHARE privilege
options.C: SECURITYADMIN
options.D: The PUBLIC role

questions.explanation: B is correct because to mount a share as a database in the consumer account, the role must have the IMPORT SHARE account-level privilege. By default, ACCOUNTADMIN has this privilege and can grant it to other roles. A is wrong because SYSADMIN does not inherently have IMPORT SHARE privilege — it must be explicitly granted. C is wrong because SECURITYADMIN handles security policies and user management, not data share imports. D is wrong because PUBLIC has no import privileges.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Consumer Tasks — Viewing and Creating a Database from a Share

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to understand why a specific query was slow. Which Snowflake feature provides a visual breakdown of execution nodes, time spent at each step, and data flow within the query?

options.A: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
options.B: Query Profile in Snowsight
options.C: EXPLAIN PLAN output
options.D: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Query Profile in Snowsight (the Snowflake web UI) provides an interactive, visual execution graph showing each operation node in the query plan, the time each node took, bytes scanned, rows produced, and spill information. It is the primary tool for diagnosing slow queries. A is wrong because QUERY_HISTORY provides overall query metadata (total duration, credits, status) but not node-level execution detail. C is wrong because EXPLAIN PLAN shows the estimated execution plan before running but does not show actual runtime statistics. D is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES shows table metadata, not query execution details.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Query Profile

questions.stem: A query that aggregates 1 billion rows is running on a Medium warehouse. The query profile shows significant 'bytes spilled to remote storage'. What is the MOST effective remediation?

options.A: Enable the Search Optimization Service on the aggregated table.
options.B: Increase the virtual warehouse size to give the query more memory.
options.C: Reduce the AUTO_SUSPEND threshold.
options.D: Rewrite the query to use a subquery instead of GROUP BY.

questions.explanation: B is correct because spilling to remote storage (the worst form of spill) occurs when in-memory and local SSD are both insufficient for intermediate aggregation data. Increasing the warehouse size provides more memory per node, allowing more of the intermediate aggregation data to stay in RAM and eliminating or reducing remote spill. A is wrong because Search Optimization Service targets selective lookup queries, not aggregations that spill. C is wrong because AUTO_SUSPEND affects idle billing, not query memory. D is wrong because GROUP BY is not the cause of spilling — the volume of data being aggregated is. Rewriting to a subquery does not reduce memory requirements.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Spilling to Remote Storage — Remediation

questions.stem: A developer creates a materialized view `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_daily_sales AS SELECT sale_date, SUM(amount) total FROM sales GROUP BY sale_date`. A new row is inserted into the `sales` table. How does the materialized view stay current?

options.A: The materialized view must be manually refreshed using ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REFRESH.
options.B: Snowflake automatically and incrementally maintains the materialized view as DML changes the base table.
options.C: The materialized view is rebuilt from scratch every hour on a background schedule.
options.D: The materialized view is invalidated and returns empty results until the next manual refresh.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake materialized views are automatically maintained by Snowflake's background services. When DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) change the base table, Snowflake incrementally updates the materialized view to reflect those changes. This maintenance is transparent to users and happens without manual intervention. A is wrong because manual refresh is not required — automatic maintenance is a core feature of Snowflake materialized views. C is wrong because maintenance is incremental and event-driven, not hourly full rebuilds. D is wrong because the materialized view is not invalidated by base table changes.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Materialized Views — Automatic Maintenance

questions.stem: Which TWO conditions must BOTH be true for Snowflake's result cache to serve a query result? (Select TWO — presented as a single-answer choice)

options.A: The exact same SQL text is submitted AND the underlying tables' data has not changed since the cached result was produced.
options.B: The query runs on the same virtual warehouse AND the result was cached within 1 hour.
options.C: The user submitting the query is the same user who originally ran it AND the warehouse size is the same.
options.D: The query contains no WHERE clause AND uses only aggregate functions.

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowflake's result cache requires (1) the query text to be semantically identical to a previously cached query, and (2) the underlying table data (and metadata) to be unchanged since the cached result was created. The result cache persists for up to 24 hours and is available account-wide — different users on different warehouses can benefit from the same cached result. B is wrong because result cache is not warehouse-specific and the TTL is 24 hours, not 1 hour. C is wrong because any user can get the cached result, not only the original submitter. D is wrong because result cache applies to any query type, including those with WHERE clauses.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Result Cache — Eligibility Requirements

questions.stem: A team is designing a large fact table that is frequently queried by date range and region. Which TWO Snowflake features work TOGETHER to minimize data scanned for these queries? (Select TWO)

options.A: CLUSTER BY (sale_date, region) to co-locate relevant micro-partitions
options.B: Multi-cluster warehouse to run more parallel queries
options.C: Automatic micro-partition pruning based on min/max metadata in clustered partitions
options.D: Increasing warehouse size to scan partitions faster

questions.explanation: A and C are correct and are inherently linked. Defining a CLUSTER BY key on (sale_date, region) ensures that data is physically co-located in micro-partitions by those columns. Snowflake's automatic partition pruning then uses the min/max metadata stored per micro-partition to skip partitions that cannot contain qualifying rows for a given date range and region filter. Together, clustering and pruning dramatically reduce the bytes scanned. B is wrong because multi-cluster adds concurrency, not scan efficiency for a single query. D is wrong because a larger warehouse scans the same number of partitions faster but does not reduce the number of partitions scanned.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Clustering Keys and Micro-Partition Pruning

questions.stem: A developer runs `ALTER TABLE large_table CLUSTER BY (customer_segment, order_date)`. The table already has 500 million rows. What happens immediately after this command?

options.A: Snowflake immediately reshuffles all 500 million rows into the new clustering order, blocking reads until complete.
options.B: The clustering key is registered but no physical reclustering occurs immediately — Snowflake's background Automatic Clustering service gradually recluster the table over time.
options.C: The command fails unless the table is empty.
options.D: Snowflake creates a new sorted copy of the table and swaps it atomically.

questions.explanation: B is correct because ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY registers the clustering key metadata immediately, but the physical reorganization of existing micro-partitions is performed asynchronously by the Automatic Clustering background service over time. New data inserted after the key is defined will also be clustered. Existing data is reclustered gradually. A is wrong because the physical reclustering is asynchronous and does not block reads. C is wrong because the command succeeds on tables with existing data. D is wrong because Snowflake does not create a full copy of the table.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Automatic Clustering

questions.stem: A data engineering team observes that their ELT pipeline creates and drops hundreds of temporary tables per hour. They notice their Snowflake bill includes significant Cloud Services charges (approaching the 10% threshold). What is the MOST likely cause?

options.A: The CREATE and DROP TABLE operations consume large amounts of compute credits.
options.B: Heavy DDL operations (CREATE/DROP TABLE) generate significant Cloud Services metadata workload, which can exceed the 10% free Cloud Services allowance.
options.C: Temporary tables have a premium storage surcharge.
options.D: Cloud Services charges are fixed and unrelated to DDL activity.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Cloud Services layer activity — including metadata operations like CREATE TABLE, DROP TABLE, SHOW, DESCRIBE, and heavy INFORMATION_SCHEMA queries — consumes Cloud Services compute. Snowflake includes Cloud Services usage up to 10% of compute credits for free; usage above 10% is billed separately. Pipelines that generate hundreds or thousands of DDL operations per hour can push Cloud Services costs above this threshold. A is wrong because DDL is not billed as compute (warehouse) credits. C is wrong because temporary tables don't have a storage surcharge. D is wrong because Cloud Services charges are variable and directly influenced by metadata operation volume.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Cloud Services Layer Billing, 10% Adjustment

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to prevent users from connecting to the account from outside the corporate network. Which object should be created and applied?

options.A: A resource monitor with IP filtering enabled.
options.B: A network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST containing corporate IP ranges, applied to the account.
options.C: A masking policy that hides data for connections outside the corporate network.
options.D: A row access policy filtering rows based on the user's source IP.

questions.explanation: B is correct because a network policy with an ALLOWED_IP_LIST containing the corporate IP ranges, when applied to the account using `ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY`, blocks all connections from IPs not in the allowed list. Users connecting from outside the corporate network cannot authenticate. A is wrong because resource monitors track credit consumption, not connection source IPs. C is wrong because masking policies hide column values but do not block connections. D is wrong because row access policies filter query results, not connection attempts.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Network Policies — Account-Level Application

questions.stem: A developer is writing a stored procedure that needs to query tables the caller does not have direct SELECT privileges on. The procedure owner has SELECT on those tables. Which stored procedure property allows the procedure to run with the owner's privileges instead of the caller's?

options.A: EXECUTE AS OWNER
options.B: EXECUTE AS CALLER
options.C: WITH GRANT OPTION
options.D: SECURITY DEFINER

questions.explanation: A is correct because `EXECUTE AS OWNER` (the default for stored procedures in Snowflake) means the procedure runs with the privileges of the procedure's owner role, not the role of the user calling the procedure. This allows the procedure to access objects the caller cannot access directly — similar to SECURITY DEFINER in PostgreSQL. B is wrong because EXECUTE AS CALLER makes the procedure run with the caller's privileges, which would fail if the caller lacks SELECT on the tables. C is wrong because WITH GRANT OPTION is a privilege grant option, not a stored procedure execution context. D is wrong because SECURITY DEFINER is not Snowflake syntax — the equivalent is EXECUTE AS OWNER.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Stored Procedures — Caller's Rights vs Owner's Rights

questions.stem: Which TWO Snowflake features are part of the Tri-Secret Secure encryption model? (Select TWO)

options.A: Snowflake-managed encryption keys for the account's data
options.B: Customer-managed keys stored in the customer's cloud key management service (e.g., AWS KMS)
options.C: A hardware security module (HSM) physically located at the customer's premises
options.D: Business Critical edition (required for Tri-Secret Secure)

questions.explanation: B and D are correct. Tri-Secret Secure is a Business Critical edition feature that combines Snowflake's managed encryption keys with a customer-managed key (CMK) stored in the customer's own cloud KMS (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, or GCP Cloud KMS). Decrypting data requires both keys, meaning Snowflake cannot access data without the customer's key. Business Critical edition is required. A is wrong because while Snowflake-managed keys are one component of standard encryption, the key differentiator of Tri-Secret Secure is the CUSTOMER-managed key component — just Snowflake-managed keys alone is not Tri-Secret Secure. C is wrong because Tri-Secret Secure uses cloud KMS, not on-premises HSMs.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Tri-Secret Secure — Business Critical Edition Feature

questions.stem: A Snowflake role hierarchy is: ACCOUNTADMIN > SYSADMIN > ANALYST > PUBLIC. If SYSADMIN owns a table, which roles can query it by default (without additional GRANT statements)?

options.A: Only SYSADMIN
options.B: SYSADMIN and ACCOUNTADMIN (roles higher in the hierarchy inherit all child role privileges)
options.C: SYSADMIN, ACCOUNTADMIN, and ANALYST
options.D: All roles including PUBLIC

questions.explanation: B is correct because in Snowflake's RBAC model, privilege inheritance flows UPWARD through the role hierarchy. ACCOUNTADMIN is the parent of SYSADMIN and inherits all of SYSADMIN's privileges, including ownership of the table. ANALYST is a child of SYSADMIN (not a parent), so it does not inherit SYSADMIN's privileges by default — privileges go up the hierarchy, not down. C is wrong because ANALYST is below SYSADMIN in the hierarchy and does not inherit upward. D is wrong because PUBLIC is the lowest role and inherits nothing from SYSADMIN.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: RBAC Role Hierarchy and Privilege Inheritance

questions.stem: A company needs to ensure that sensitive column values in a shared table are masked differently based on whether the querying user is an internal employee or an external consumer. Which Snowflake feature combination achieves this?

options.A: Row Access Policy filtering on IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() combined with a secure view.
options.B: A Dynamic Data Masking policy that evaluates CURRENT_ROLE() or IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() to return full or masked values.
options.C: Two separate tables — one with full data for internal users and one pre-masked for external consumers.
options.D: Column-level encryption using customer-managed keys with role-specific decryption.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Dynamic Data Masking policies can include conditional logic using CURRENT_ROLE() or IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() to return the full value for trusted internal roles and a masked value for external consumer roles. Since masking policies apply transparently at query time, the same shared table serves both audiences with appropriate data visibility. A is wrong because row access policies filter rows, not column values — combining with a secure view would work but doesn't specifically address column value masking per consumer type. C is wrong because maintaining two separate tables adds operational complexity and data synchronization risk. D is wrong because column-level encryption with per-role decryption is not a native Snowflake feature.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Dynamic Data Masking — Role-Based Conditional Logic

questions.stem: A developer uses `SELECT PARSE_JSON('{"score": 95}'):score`. What does this expression return?

options.A: The integer 95 as a NUMBER data type.
options.B: The VARIANT value 95 (number within a VARIANT container).
options.C: The string '95' as a VARCHAR.
options.D: An error because colon notation requires a column reference, not an inline PARSE_JSON call.

questions.explanation: B is correct because PARSE_JSON() parses a JSON string and returns a VARIANT. Applying :score (colon notation) to the VARIANT navigates to the 'score' key and returns the value as a VARIANT. The result is a VARIANT containing the numeric value 95, not a native NUMBER. To get a NUMBER, you would need to cast: `PARSE_JSON('{"score": 95}'):score::NUMBER`. A is wrong because the result is VARIANT, not NUMBER, without an explicit cast. C is wrong because no cast to STRING is applied. D is wrong because colon notation is valid on any VARIANT expression, not just column references.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: PARSE_JSON, Colon Notation for VARIANT

questions.stem: A table has a VARIANT column `payload` containing: `{"orders": [{"id": 1, "item": "pen"}, {"id": 2, "item": "book"}]}`. Which query returns each order as a separate row with columns `order_id` and `item_name`?

options.A: SELECT payload:orders:id, payload:orders:item FROM events;
options.B: SELECT f.value:id::NUMBER AS order_id, f.value:item::STRING AS item_name FROM events, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => payload:orders) f;
options.C: SELECT UNNEST(payload:orders) FROM events;
options.D: SELECT GET_PATH(payload, 'orders[*].id'), GET_PATH(payload, 'orders[*].item') FROM events;

questions.explanation: B is correct because LATERAL FLATTEN on the `orders` array expands each array element into a row, and `f.value` refers to each element (a JSON object). Extracting `:id` and `:item` with appropriate type casts gives the desired result set. A is wrong because colon notation on an array without flattening returns the entire array, not individual elements. C is wrong because UNNEST is not a Snowflake function — FLATTEN is the equivalent. D is wrong because GET_PATH with array wildcard `[*]` is not valid Snowflake GET_PATH syntax and would not produce multiple rows.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Querying Semi-Structured Data — LATERAL FLATTEN with Nested Arrays

questions.stem: A Snowflake table has a VARIANT column containing JSON data. A developer runs `SELECT v:name::STRING FROM json_table WHERE v:status::STRING = 'active'`. Snowflake runs this query efficiently without scanning all rows. What Snowflake optimization enables this?

options.A: Search Optimization Service must be enabled for VARIANT path pruning.
options.B: Snowflake stores metadata (min/max per VARIANT path) within each micro-partition, enabling pruning on VARIANT paths just as on structured columns.
options.C: Automatic clustering on VARIANT columns is applied by default.
options.D: Snowflake converts all VARIANT data to structured columns at query time using schema-on-read.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake's micro-partition metadata includes statistics for commonly accessed VARIANT paths (virtual columns). This allows Snowflake to prune micro-partitions based on VARIANT path predicates similarly to regular column predicates, without loading the full JSON from all partitions. A is wrong because basic VARIANT path pruning is built into micro-partition metadata — the Search Optimization Service provides additional optimization for highly selective equality lookups. C is wrong because clustering on VARIANT columns is not automatic. D is wrong because Snowflake does not convert VARIANT to structured columns — it uses the stored VARIANT with path-level metadata.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Semi-Structured Data Optimization — Virtual Columns

questions.stem: Which TWO functions can be used to safely handle missing keys in a VARIANT document in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.A: TRY_PARSE_JSON() — returns NULL instead of an error if the input is not valid JSON.
options.B: GET() — returns NULL (not an error) when the key does not exist in the VARIANT.
options.C: COALESCE() — automatically retries with an alternative key path if the first is missing.
options.D: OBJECT_KEYS() — prevents errors by listing all available keys before access.

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. TRY_PARSE_JSON() safely parses a JSON string and returns NULL if the input is malformed or NULL, rather than raising an error. GET(variant_col, 'key') returns NULL when the key does not exist in the VARIANT object — this is the safe behavior of both GET() and colon/bracket notation in Snowflake (accessing a missing key returns NULL VARIANT, not an error). C is wrong because COALESCE() evaluates expressions left to right and returns the first non-NULL, but it does not retry alternative key paths — both paths would still be evaluated. D is wrong because OBJECT_KEYS() lists keys but does not prevent access errors by itself.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: TRY_PARSE_JSON, GET Function — Handling Missing Keys

questions.stem: A developer creates a table: `CREATE TABLE events (id INT, raw VARIANT)`. They load JSON data with STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE. Later they run: `SELECT TYPEOF(raw:amount) FROM events`. Some rows return 'REAL', some return 'INTEGER', and some return 'NULL_VALUE'. What explains this?

options.A: Snowflake requires all VARIANT keys to have the same type; the mixed types indicate a load error.
options.B: VARIANT columns are schema-less — different rows can have different types for the same key path, and TYPEOF() reflects the actual stored type per row.
options.C: TYPEOF() returns 'NULL_VALUE' only for rows where the raw column itself is NULL.
options.D: REAL and INTEGER both mean numeric; the difference is a display artifact with no functional significance.

questions.explanation: B is correct because VARIANT is schema-less in Snowflake. Different rows can store different JSON types for the same key path — some rows may have `amount` as a decimal (stored as REAL), others as an integer (stored as INTEGER), and others may be missing the key entirely (TYPEOF returns 'NULL_VALUE' when the key is absent or the value is JSON null). TYPEOF() reports the actual type of the value in each row's VARIANT. A is wrong because VARIANT explicitly allows mixed types — it is a feature, not an error. C is wrong because 'NULL_VALUE' specifically refers to a JSON null value or missing key, not a NULL VARIANT column. D is wrong because REAL and INTEGER are distinct type representations that can affect arithmetic and comparisons.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: TYPEOF Function, VARIANT Schema-less Typing

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `GET @my_stage/results.csv file:///tmp/results.csv`. What does this command do?

options.A: Uploads the local file `/tmp/results.csv` to the Snowflake internal stage.
options.B: Downloads the file `results.csv` from the internal stage to the local path `/tmp/results.csv`.
options.C: Copies the file from the internal stage to an external S3 bucket.
options.D: Validates that the file in the stage matches the local file checksum.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the GET command downloads a file from a Snowflake internal (or external) stage to the local file system. The syntax is `GET @<stage>/<path> file://<local_path>`. This is the complement to PUT, which uploads files. A is wrong because uploading uses PUT, not GET. C is wrong because GET downloads to local file system, not to another cloud storage location. D is wrong because GET is a data transfer command, not a validation command.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: GET Command — Downloading Files from Stages

questions.stem: A team uses Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE on an S3 external stage. A new file arrives in S3. What is the complete sequence of events that causes the file to be loaded?

options.A: Snowflake polls S3 every minute, detects the new file, and triggers ingestion.
options.B: S3 sends an event notification to an SQS queue; Snowpipe reads the SQS message, retrieves the file from S3, and loads it into the target table.
options.C: The user manually runs REFRESH SNOWPIPE to trigger ingestion of new files.
options.D: Azure Event Grid sends a notification to Snowflake when the file arrives.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowpipe's AUTO_INGEST on AWS works via S3 event notifications. When a file is written to the monitored S3 prefix, S3 sends an event notification to an AWS SQS queue that was configured when the Snowpipe was created. Snowflake's Snowpipe service continuously monitors this SQS queue, picks up the notification, retrieves the file from S3, and loads it into the target table — typically within a minute. A is wrong because Snowpipe does not poll S3 on a timer; it is event-driven via SQS. C is wrong because the entire pipeline is automated when AUTO_INGEST = TRUE; manual REFRESH is used when AUTO_INGEST is false. D is wrong because Azure Event Grid is the equivalent mechanism on Azure, not on AWS S3.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Snowpipe AUTO_INGEST — S3 Event Notification Flow

questions.stem: A COPY INTO command uses `FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV SKIP_HEADER = 1 NULL_IF = ('NULL','\\N') EMPTY_FIELD_AS_NULL = TRUE)`. What does EMPTY_FIELD_AS_NULL = TRUE do?

options.A: Skips rows where any field is empty.
options.B: Treats empty string fields ('') in the CSV as SQL NULL values in the target table.
options.C: Converts NULL values in the table to empty strings when exporting.
options.D: Sets the default value for all NULL fields to an empty string.

questions.explanation: B is correct because `EMPTY_FIELD_AS_NULL = TRUE` instructs Snowflake to interpret empty string fields (two consecutive delimiters with nothing between them) as SQL NULL values during loading. Without this option, an empty field might be loaded as an empty string or cause a type conversion error for non-string columns. A is wrong because rows are not skipped — the empty fields are converted to NULL. C is wrong because EMPTY_FIELD_AS_NULL only affects loading (COPY INTO to table), not unloading. D is wrong because this option converts empty fields to NULL, not the reverse.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: CSV File Format Options — EMPTY_FIELD_AS_NULL

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to load data from an external S3 stage but wants each file's source name to be stored in the target table alongside the loaded data. Which COPY INTO feature provides this?

options.A: INCLUDE_METADATA option with the FILENAME metadata column.
options.B: The METADATA$FILENAME pseudocolumn available in COPY INTO SELECT.
options.C: Adding a DEFAULT expression `DEFAULT CURRENT_FILE()` to the target table column.
options.D: This is not possible in Snowflake — source file names cannot be captured during COPY INTO.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake exposes pseudocolumns like METADATA$FILENAME and METADATA$FILE_ROW_NUMBER that are available when selecting from a stage. Using `COPY INTO table FROM (SELECT METADATA$FILENAME, $1, $2, ... FROM @my_stage)` allows the source file name to be captured and stored in a target column. A is wrong because INCLUDE_METADATA is not a standard COPY INTO option for capturing filenames. C is wrong because CURRENT_FILE() is not a valid Snowflake default expression. D is wrong because it is possible via the COPY INTO SELECT with METADATA$ pseudocolumns.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: COPY INTO — METADATA$FILENAME Pseudocolumn

questions.stem: Which command shows the clustering information (Average Depth, Average Overlap Depth) for a Snowflake table to assess how well-clustered it is?

options.A: DESCRIBE TABLE orders;
options.B: SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('orders', '(order_date)');
options.C: SHOW CLUSTERING KEYS IN TABLE orders;
options.D: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2024-01-01';

questions.explanation: B is correct because SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION() is the Snowflake system function that returns detailed clustering statistics for a table, including Average Depth and Average Overlap Depth for a given column or set of columns. These metrics indicate how well the data is physically ordered relative to the specified key. A is wrong because DESCRIBE TABLE shows column definitions, not clustering statistics. C is wrong because SHOW CLUSTERING KEYS shows what clustering keys are defined but not the clustering depth metrics. D is wrong because EXPLAIN shows the query plan, not table clustering statistics.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION

questions.stem: A Snowflake Enterprise account has a table with `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 30`. An administrator runs `ALTER TABLE orders SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0`. What are the implications?

options.A: Time Travel is disabled for the table; historical data from before the change is immediately purged.
options.B: Time Travel is disabled for future changes; existing Time Travel data (up to 30 days) is gradually purged, and Fail-Safe still applies for 7 days after expiry.
options.C: The command is rejected because you cannot reduce Time Travel below 1 day on Enterprise edition.
options.D: Setting retention to 0 moves all historical data to Fail-Safe immediately for 30 days.

questions.explanation: B is correct because setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for future changes to the table. Historical data that was accumulated during the 30-day retention period is not immediately purged — it is gradually expired according to when each micro-partition's time travel window closes. Fail-Safe (7 days) still applies after each micro-partition's Time Travel window expires. A is wrong because historical data is not immediately purged — it expires as the time windows close. C is wrong because reducing to 0 is allowed even on Enterprise edition. D is wrong because Fail-Safe is not activated immediately — it begins after each micro-partition's Time Travel window closes.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Changing Time Travel Retention — Effect on Existing Data

questions.stem: A developer uses `CREATE TABLE dev_orders CLONE prod_orders`. The cloned table is used for development testing and has many rows deleted and updated. Which statement about storage costs is TRUE?

options.A: The clone and original share all storage costs; no additional storage is ever charged.
options.B: Storage charges accumulate only for micro-partitions that have been modified in either the clone or the original — shared unmodified partitions are counted once.
options.C: The clone immediately doubles storage costs because a full copy is made.
options.D: Deleted rows in the clone reduce storage costs for the provider immediately.

questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake's zero-copy clone uses copy-on-write semantics. Micro-partitions that are unchanged in both the clone and the original are shared and counted once in storage. New micro-partitions are created and charged separately only when data is modified in either the clone or the original. So storage costs increase incrementally as changes accumulate, not immediately. A is wrong because modifications to either the clone or original do create new micro-partitions that are charged. C is wrong because zero-copy clone creates no duplicate of unchanged data at clone time. D is wrong because deleted rows in Snowflake are still stored until their Time Travel and Fail-Safe periods expire — deletion does not immediately free storage.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Zero-Copy Clone Storage Costs

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider wants to publish a dataset on the Snowflake Marketplace that allows consumers to instantly get access without requesting approval. Which listing type should they choose?

options.A: Private Listing (direct share to specific accounts only)
options.B: Auto-fulfillment Listing with request-and-approval workflow
options.C: Free Instant Listing with auto-fulfillment enabled
options.D: Paid Listing with contract-based access

questions.explanation: C is correct because a Free Instant Listing on the Snowflake Marketplace with auto-fulfillment allows any Snowflake consumer to discover the listing and immediately get access to the data without a request/approval step. The data is shared live via Snowflake's native sharing mechanism. A is wrong because private listings are shared only with specific, named consumer accounts and require the provider to explicitly add each consumer. B is wrong because a request-and-approval workflow requires the provider to manually review and approve each access request — not instant. D is wrong because paid listings require consumers to agree to terms and complete a purchase, which is not instant auto-access.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Marketplace Listing Types — Free vs Paid, Instant Access

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider shares a database using a Data Exchange. Which TWO actions can the data CONSUMER perform with the shared database? (Select TWO)

options.A: Run SELECT queries against the shared tables and views.
options.B: Create a zero-copy clone of the shared database to their own account.
options.C: Create local views in the consumer account that reference the shared tables.
options.D: Run INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statements on the shared tables.

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Consumers of a shared database can query (SELECT) the tables and views in the share. They can also create views in their own database that reference the shared tables — this is a common pattern for adding consumer-side transformations or aliasing without copying data. B is wrong because consumers cannot zero-copy clone a shared database into their own account — the shared database is read-only and does not belong to the consumer's account. D is wrong because shared databases are read-only to consumers — INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE are not permitted on provider-owned shared data.
questions.reference: Snowflake Documentation: Data Consumer Tasks — Querying Shared Data
